flashcards.front: What is Union Find (Disjoint Set Union)?
flashcards.back: A data structure that tracks elements partitioned into disjoint subsets. It supports two main operations: find (determine which subset an element belongs to) and union (merge two subsets). Used for network connectivity, cycle detection, and image processing.
Returns the representative (root) of the set containing a given element. It recursively follows parent pointers until reaching the root. Without optimization, this takes O(n) time in the worst case.

Merges two disjoint sets by making one root a child of the other. The basic version simply attaches one root to another without considering tree height. It runs in O(1) time plus the cost of find operations.

An optimization that flattens the tree structure during find operations by making each node point directly to the root. After accessing a node, path compression recursively updates all intermediate nodes' parents to the root.

flashcards.front: Union by Rank in Union Find
flashcards.back: An optimization that attaches the smaller tree under the root of the larger tree. Each node maintains a rank (approximate tree height), and union by rank ensures the resulting tree remains balanced.

With both path compression and union by rank, the amortized time complexity is O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function. This is effectively constant time (≤4 for all practical n) for m operations on n elements.

Used in Kruskal's algorithm for Minimum Spanning Tree, detecting cycles in undirected graphs, network connectivity in distributed systems, image segmentation, and Lagrange's union-find trick in combinatorics.

class UnionFind: def __init__(self, n): self.parent = list(range(n)); self.rank = [0]*n. def find(self, x): return x if self.parent[x]==x else self.find(self.parent[x]). def union(self, x, y): rx, ry = self.find(x), self.find(y); if rx!=ry: self.parent[ry] = rx if self.rank[rx]>self.rank[ry] else self.parent[rx]=ry.
